mnemonics.title: GUST - Types of Transmission Media
mnemonics.content: GUST stands for Guided, Unguided, Signals, Transmission. Guided media (wired) includes Twisted Pair, Coaxial, Fiber. Unguided (wireless) includes Radio, Microwave, Satellite.

mnemonics.title: TeCoF - Guided Media Types
mnemonics.content: TeCoF = Twisted Pair, Coaxial, Fiber - the three main guided/wired transmission media in order of increasing bandwidth and cost.

mnemonics.title: A.D. P.N. - Signal Types
mnemonics.content: A.D. = Analog Data, P.N. = Digital Data. Analog signals are continuous waves; Digital signals are discrete pulses (0s and 1s).

mnemonics.title: S.H.F. - Transmission Impairments
mnemonics.content: S.H.F. = Signal loss (Attenuation), Harmonic distortion, Foreign noise (Interference). These are the three main causes of signal degradation.

mnemonics.title: R.M.I.S. - Unguided Media
mnemonics.content: R.M.I.S. = Radio, Microwave, Infrared, Satellite - the four types of wireless/unguided transmission media.

mnemonics.title: T.P.C.S. - Twisted Pair Categories
mnemonics.content: T.P.C.S. = Telephone (Cat 1), Printer (Cat 3), Computer (Cat 5), Switch (Cat 6) - categories increasing in speed from 4 Mbps to 10 Gbps.

acronyms.usage: Most common Ethernet cable, used in LANs, susceptible to EMI. Use when cost is a factor and no heavy interference.
Twisted pair with shielding, used in environments with high EMI. Use when protection from interference is needed.
Higher SNR = clearer signal. Calculate by dividing signal power by noise power. Used to determine signal quality.
Ratio of error bits to total bits transmitted. Lower BER = better quality. Used to measure digital transmission accuracy.
Required for microwave and satellite communication. Obstructions block signal. Use in open areas for wireless transmission.
External interference from electrical devices. Higher in unshielded cables. Consider when routing cables near motors or fluorescent lights.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a smooth hill (analog) as a continuous wave versus stairs (digital) as discrete steps. The hill flows smoothly like water, stairs are stepped like boxes.
Picture a flashlight getting dimmer as you walk away from it. The signal weakens over distance, just like light fades.
Think of two wires twisted around each other like a DNA helix. The twisting cancels out interference, like how two swimmers circling each other create balance.
Imagine water flowing through a pipe at 90-degree angles, bouncing off walls (total internal reflection). Light pulses bounce through the glass fiber.
Think of a highway: more lanes = more cars (data) can pass. Higher bandwidth = wider highway = more data transmission capacity.
Picture a garden hose with layers: inner water (core), plastic insulation, metal shielding, outer cover. Same structure - center conductor, insulator, shield, jacket.

analogies.analogies: Guided media is like sending a letter through a postal pipe (physical path), unguided media is like throwing a ball to someone across the field (no physical path, travels through air).
Baseband is like a single-lane road where one car uses the entire road (entire bandwidth for one signal), broadband is like a multi-lane highway where multiple cars (signals) travel simultaneously.
Like two people rowing a boat - one on left, one on right, strokes cancel each other's wobble. Equal and opposite interference signals cancel out in the twists.
Like sending light through a mirror-lined tunnel - it bounces at speed of light with no heat loss. Faster than electricity through copper wires.
Like trying to hear someone in a crowded room. The conversation (signal) gets masked by background chatter (noise). SNR tells you how audible the conversation is.
Simplex = one-way street (only cars go one direction). Half-duplex = single-lane bridge (cars take turns). Full-duplex = two-way street (cars go both ways simultaneously).
